name: NetworkParsing
tagline: Fetch JSON from a real API with Retrofit, parse it, and render explicit Loading / Success / Error states.
whatItDoes: The app opens on a single screen that immediately starts fetching a list of notes. While the request runs you see a centered spinner with 'Loading notes...'. On success the notes appear in a scrolling list, each showing a bold title and a body paragraph separated by dividers. If the load fails you instead see a 'Couldn't load notes' message, the error reason, and a Retry button that re-runs the fetch. By default the app runs offline against a fake repository, so it always succeeds after a ~600ms delay.
mentalModel: A network screen is never just 'the data' - at every instant it is in exactly one of Loading, Success, or Error, so model those states as a sealed type, expose the current one as a StateFlow, and make the UI a pure function of that state.

screens.name: NotesScreen
screens.role: Stateful entry composable: grabs the NotesViewModel, collects its uiState StateFlow lifecycle-awarely, and hands the state plus a retry callback to NotesContent.

screens.name: NotesContent
screens.role: Stateless renderer with one exhaustive 'when' over NotesUiState that draws the spinner, the list, or the error+Retry screen.

screens.name: LoadingState
screens.role: Centers a CircularProgressIndicator with a 'Loading notes...' label while the request is in flight.

screens.name: NotesList
screens.role: Renders the Success state as a LazyColumn of notes, each row showing title and body with a HorizontalDivider between them.

screens.name: ErrorState
screens.role: Renders the Error state: a heading, the failure message, and a Retry Button wired to onRetry.

concepts.plain: NoteDto is the exact JSON wire shape (it has 'userId') and carries the @Serializable annotation; Note is the clean shape the app actually uses and deliberately drops 'userId'. Keeping them separate means the UI never depends on the API's exact format.

@Serializable on NoteDto tells the kotlinx.serialization compiler plugin to generate a decoder, so raw JSON text like {'userId':1,'id':1,'title':...} can be turned into a NoteDto automatically - the field names match the JSON keys by name.

NoteApi is just an interface where @GET('posts') and 'suspend fun fetchNotes(): List<NoteDto>' describe an HTTP call; provideNoteApi() builds the real implementation at runtime from a baseUrl plus the kotlinx.serialization converter. You never write socket or URL code.

NoteRepository is an interface with one method getNotes(). RealNoteRepository hits the network; FakeNoteRepository parses a hardcoded JSON string offline after a delay. provideNoteRepository(useFake) is the single switch between them.

NotesUiState is a sealed interface with exactly three subtypes - the Loading data object, Success(notes), and Error(message). Because it is sealed the compiler can verify a 'when' handles every case with no 'else'.

The ViewModel keeps a private MutableStateFlow named _uiState that only it can write, and exposes it publicly as a read-only StateFlow via .asStateFlow(). The screen can observe it but never mutate it.

loadNotes() sets Loading, then inside a try calls repository.getNotes() and publishes Success; any thrown exception is caught and turned into Error(message). This single try/catch is the one place a network failure becomes a visible state instead of a crash.
collectAsStateWithLifecycle() reads the StateFlow but pauses collection when the screen is not visible, unlike a plain collectAsState(). This is the safe way to observe a flow from Compose.

files.role: The Compose UI layer: MainActivity entry point, the stateful NotesScreen, the stateless NotesContent and its child composables, plus @Preview functions for each state.
The parsing boundary: the @Serializable NoteDto (JSON shape), the plain Note domain model, and the NoteDto.toNote() mapper.
The Retrofit NoteApi interface (@GET('posts')) and provideNoteApi() which assembles Retrofit from a baseUrl plus the kotlinx.serialization JSON converter.
The NoteRepository interface plus RealNoteRepository (network), FakeNoteRepository (offline hardcoded JSON), and provideNoteRepository(useFake) - the online/offline switch.
The NotesUiState sealed interface and the NotesViewModel that owns the StateFlow and the loadNotes() logic.
Project overview explaining the DTO-vs-domain, Retrofit, UiState, error handling, and Real-vs-Fake concepts plus how to run it online or offline.

highlights.title: The @Serializable DTO mirrors the JSON keys exactly
highlights.explain: @Serializable is the one annotation that makes JSON decoding possible - it triggers the kotlinx.serialization compiler plugin to generate a serializer for NoteDto. Each property (userId, id, title, body) matches a JSON key by name and type, so a JSON object decodes straight into a NoteDto. If a key ever differed from the property name you would add @SerialName, but here the names already line up.

highlights.title: The mapper: the single network-shape to app-shape boundary
highlights.explain: NoteDto.toNote() is an extension function so it reads like dto.toNote(). It copies id, title, and body across but intentionally drops userId, which the UI never needs. Centralizing the conversion here means if the API adds or renames fields, only NoteDto and this one mapper change - never the screens or ViewModel.

highlights.title: Retrofit describes the endpoint as a suspend interface method
highlights.explain: You only declare the contract: @GET('posts') marks fetchNotes() as an HTTP GET of the relative path appended to baseUrl, and the return type List<NoteDto> tells Retrofit plus the converter to decode the JSON array for you. 'suspend' means the call is a coroutine - Retrofit runs the network I/O off the main thread and resumes you with the parsed list, no callbacks or manual threading.

highlights.title: Assembling Retrofit with the kotlinx.serialization converter
highlights.explain: provideNoteApi() wires three things: a baseUrl that must end in '/', addConverterFactory which plugs the json engine in as the (de)serializer, and create(NoteApi::class.java) which makes Retrofit generate the concrete implementation. The shared 'json = Json { ignoreUnknownKeys = true }' on line 60 means new server fields will not break parsing - the single most important real-world robustness setting.

highlights.title: The Real-vs-Fake switch in one line
highlights.explain: provideNoteRepository(useFake) is the one place that decides whether the app is online or offline. With useFake = true (the default) it returns FakeNoteRepository, which parses a hardcoded JSON string after a delay so the app and unit tests run with no internet; flip it to false to get RealNoteRepository hitting the live API. The ViewModel depends only on the NoteRepository interface, so neither it nor the UI changes.

highlights.title: The sealed UiState: three states the screen can be in
highlights.explain: NotesUiState is a sealed interface with exactly three cases: Loading (a data object singleton with no data), Success(notes), and Error(message). Because the set of subtypes is closed, a 'when' over a NotesUiState can be exhaustive with no 'else' - the compiler forces you to handle every state, so you physically cannot forget one.

highlights.title: StateFlow plus the try/catch loading logic
highlights.explain: loadNotes() launches in viewModelScope (auto-cancelled when the VM clears, never blocking the main thread), sets _uiState to Loading, then in a try calls the suspending repository.getNotes() and publishes Success. Any exception - no network, bad JSON, timeout - is caught and becomes Error(message). The private _uiState (line 80) is written only here while the public uiState (line 84) is exposed read-only via asStateFlow().

highlights.title: Stateless rendering: one exhaustive when over the state
highlights.explain: NotesContent holds no state and creates no ViewModel - it just takes a NotesUiState and an onRetry lambda and maps each case to a composable: Loading to a spinner, Success to NotesList, Error to ErrorState with the Retry wired to onRetry. Because the input is the sealed NotesUiState, this is exactly why the @Previews can drive it directly with hand-made states, and why the UI is a pure function of state.

newVsPrev: ComposeLists taught you to render an in-memory list with LazyColumn and items. NetworkParsing keeps that list UI but adds the whole data-fetching stack underneath it: a real HTTP call via Retrofit (@GET, suspend fetchNotes), JSON parsing with kotlinx.serialization (@Serializable NoteDto), a DTO-to-domain mapper, a NoteRepository abstraction with swappable Real/Fake implementations, and a NotesViewModel that exposes a sealed NotesUiState (Loading/Success/Error) as a StateFlow collected with collectAsStateWithLifecycle(). In short: the list is no longer hardcoded - it now arrives asynchronously, can fail, and the UI handles all three outcomes.
tryThis: In NoteRepository.kt change provideNoteRepository's default to useFake = false, run on a device WITH internet, and watch the real https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts data load (100 posts instead of the 3 offline ones).
Still with useFake = false, turn on airplane mode and relaunch: the try/catch in loadNotes() should produce the Error state - tap Retry after re-enabling the network to see it recover.
In FakeNoteRepository increase the delay(600) to delay(3000) so the Loading spinner stays visible for three seconds, confirming the Loading state is real and not instant.
Add a new field to NoteDto, for example 'val fakeExtra: Int = 0', then add a matching key in the hardcoded FAKE_NOTES_JSON and surface it in the list row, to practice extending the parse boundary.
Edit FAKE_NOTES_JSON to be malformed (delete a closing brace) and observe that the decode throws and the ViewModel turns it into the Error state rather than crashing.
gotchas: The app runs OFFLINE by default (useFake = true), so what you see comes from the hardcoded FAKE_NOTES_JSON string, not the network - the fake still parses real JSON and adds a delay so the flow is identical, but you must flip the switch to actually exercise Retrofit.

The README says the INTERNET permission is commented out and needs uncommenting for live mode, but the real AndroidManifest.xml declares <uses-permission android:name='android.permission.INTERNET' /> ACTIVE on line 11 - so going live only requires flipping useFake to false, no manifest edit.

NoteDto.userId is parsed (so decoding the JSON does not fail) but is deliberately NOT carried into the Note domain model - that omission is the entire point of having a separate domain model, not an oversight.

The @Preview functions never construct a real NotesViewModel; they drive the stateless NotesContent with hand-made NotesUiState values, because a ViewModel would try to run a coroutine / hit the repository that the design pane cannot execute.
runTest: Build and run from the project root with ./gradlew installDebug (or ./gradlew assembleDebug --console=plain --stacktrace to just compile). Launch the app: by default it runs offline, so you should briefly see the 'Loading notes...' spinner (~600ms) then a list of three 'Offline note' rows. To exercise the live path, set provideNoteRepository(useFake = false) in NoteRepository.kt and run on a networked device to load the real /posts data; toggle airplane mode to see the Error state and Retry button. Run the unit tests (mapper + offline fake) with ./gradlew testDebugUnitTest - they need no internet.
